Event: He temporarily fills in as City Manager during the budget crisis
Explanation: Steps into leadership in a crisis, stabilizing the city and advancing his own career.

Event: He takes the City Manager position permanently
Explanation: Secures a powerful role he’s good at, benefiting both himself and the administration.

Event: He refuses to play favorites in government
Explanation: Maintains fairness and integrity, preserving trust and his own credibility.

Event: He makes time to listen to Parks Department problems
Explanation: Invests effort to support subordinates, building loyalty and solving issues.

Event: He reminds the Parks Department of his authority when needed
Explanation: Uses authority to ensure necessary work gets done, with some friction but net benefit.

Event: He offers Ron the assistant city manager position
Explanation: Gives Ron a career opportunity and potential organizational support.

Event: He tells Ron he must actually lead the department if he stays
Explanation: Holds Ron accountable, improving leadership behavior despite short-term discomfort.

Event: He launches a formal investigation into Leslie and Ben’s relationship
Explanation: Protects institutional integrity and himself at significant cost and stress to the couple.

Event: He refuses to half‑ass Leslie’s ethics trial despite caring for them
Explanation: Overzealous rigor hurts them more than it helps anyone, while preserving his rule-bound identity.

Event: He clearly warns Tammy Two not to lie under oath
Explanation: Deters perjury, safeguarding the process and his own legal/ethical position.

Event: He intimidates Tammy Two into recanting and leaving
Explanation: Removes a dishonest witness, protecting the trial and himself from tainted evidence.

Event: He prepares to physically confront Jamm at the wedding
Explanation: Defending Leslie’s honor would emotionally satisfy him but escalate harm; Ron preempts it.

Event: He scolds the Parks Department for goofing off instead of fixing the fountain
Explanation: Applies negative feedback to restore focus and responsibility.

Event: He makes a vegetable loaf instead of cake for April and Andy’s party
Explanation: Imposes his health agenda, gaining self‑satisfaction while disappointing everyone else.

Event: He later criticizes Ron for ruining the employee appreciation barbecue
Explanation: Points out misaligned priorities, improving future events despite prior hypocrisy.

Event: He delegates bad‑news delivery to Ben as a strategy
Explanation: Shields himself from unpopularity while shifting reputational damage onto Ben.

Event: He asks Ben “is there anything we can do about that?” then leaves him with the bad news
Explanation: Retains a benevolent image while forcing Ben to be the visible naysayer.

Event: He adopts a hyper-polite full‑name, pointing greeting with new people
Explanation: Builds memorable rapport though occasionally off‑putting, mildly helping himself and others.

Event: He accepts Andy’s admiration that he’d “pick a dude” as flattering
Explanation: Responds positively, strengthening social bonds with no downside.

Event: He is openly flattered when Jean-Ralphio suggests a threesome
Explanation: Takes the compliment without escalating awkwardness, preserving social ease.

Event: He warmly accepts Milton calling him a “very pretty man”
Explanation: Handles an odd remark graciously, smoothing interaction.

Event: He pushes intense health habits (10k, vitamins, strict diet)
Explanation: Greatly improves his own health, with some secondhand benefit via example.

Event: He replaces party cake with a “healthier” option for others’ good
Explanation: Attempts to improve their health but mainly imposes preferences and ruins enjoyment.

Event: He implements a town-wide health initiative
Explanation: Uses policy to improve public health in line with his values.

Event: He tries to help everyone with any problem, even when he doesn’t understand it
Explanation: Overhelping often stresses him and makes others’ situations worse.

Event: He learns to sometimes just say “That sucks” and listen to Ann
Explanation: Adjusts behavior to meet her emotional needs, improving the relationship and easing his own burden.

Event: He breaks up with Ann through an obscure feng shui metaphor
Explanation: Causes confusion and hurt without benefiting himself; the breakup is mishandled and delayed.

Event: He deliberately seeks a contrasting love interest (Shauna) to break his pattern
Explanation: Consciously expands his dating scope, likely improving his own growth and her experience.

Event: He dates Shauna despite her liking red meat
Explanation: Accepts differences, gaining a relationship and showing flexibility.

Event: He acts as a matchmaker for Ben
Explanation: Invests effort to improve Ben’s love life, gaining satisfaction from helping.

Event: He enforces a strict “no office relationships” rule on Ben and Leslie
Explanation: Protects rules/organization but harms them emotionally and professionally.

Event: He drinks Lagavulin on occasion despite his usual avoidance of alcohol
Explanation: Allows limited indulgence, modest personal benefit with negligible effect on others.

Event: He substitutes a vegetable loaf for cake on health grounds (noted hypocrisy context)
Explanation: Again imposes health values in a celebratory context, benefiting only his principles.

Event: He tries to remove hamburgers from the commissary in favor of a turkey burger
Explanation: Pursues a health goal that conflicts with widespread preference and enjoyment.

Event: He bets Ron his turkey burger can beat a beef burger
Explanation: Seeks validation of his worldview; loses socially when proven wrong.

Event: He honestly acknowledges that Ron’s beef burger is better
Explanation: Admits defeat, aligning with reality and social goodwill.

Event: He pushes himself to physical extremes, causing flu and electrolyte vulnerability
Explanation: Extreme optimization backfires on his own health with no clear gain for others.

Event: He avoids “poisoning his body” with alcohol almost entirely
Explanation: Protects long‑term health with minor social trade‑offs.

Event: He insists on involving Garry closely in courting Millicent out of respect
Explanation: Strengthens trust with her father, supporting family dynamics and his own image.

Event: He tells Garry whenever Millicent sleeps over
Explanation: Overshares intimate details, making everyone uncomfortable without benefit.

Event: He persistently pursues Ann when she’s initially not interested
Explanation: Invests in getting what he wants while creating pressure and awkwardness for her.

Event: He moves on quickly after breaking up with Ann
Explanation: Protects his own emotional state while intensifying her post‑breakup pain.

Event: He later pines for Ann while she dates Tom, spiraling into depression
Explanation: Harms his own mental health while not materially affecting others.

Event: He starts dating Shauna, making Ann jealous
Explanation: Improves his own love life but triggers Ann’s jealousy and discomfort.

Event: He gives every guy his own ideal bachelor party when he learns they never had one
Explanation: Spends effort/resources to create joy and closure for multiple friends, gaining fulfillment.

Event: He applies strict non-favoritism to Ben in rule enforcement
Explanation: Protects institutional fairness, even at some personal/relational cost.

Event: He listens to Ron’s expertise where he lacks it
Explanation: Defers appropriately, improving outcomes and deepening mutual respect.

Event: He scares off Orin with overwhelming positivity
Explanation: Rids the space of a disturbing presence with relatively harmless cheerfulness.

Event: He uses constant movement and mental occupation to avoid despair
Explanation: Short‑term coping preserves function but sustains underlying psychological harm to himself.

Event: He loads up on herbal remedies during Leslie’s trial, like an addict
Explanation: Attempts self‑medication that worsens his own physical and mental state.

Event: He refuses to bend rules as a Principles Zealot, even when he wants to
Explanation: Clinging to form over substance stresses him and harms others without clear net gain.

Event: He tells Tammy 2 he will have her charged and fired if she lies
Explanation: Strong deterrent to corruption, protecting both his role and institutional integrity.

Event: He leaves Pawnee with Ann to start their family
Explanation: Chooses personal life and family stability, with the town losing him but net family benefit.

Event: He expresses that he has to go to the bathroom twelve times a night
Explanation: His hyper‑tuned body severely disrupts his own comfort without clear benefit to anyone.

Event: He continues to investigate Leslie and Ben fully despite hating the role
Explanation: Sticks to a painful process that harms him emotionally and doesn’t clearly help others.

Event: He holds witnesses to high standards even if their lies would help his case
Explanation: Sacrifices an easy win to maintain justice and institutional legitimacy.
